# Scorched-Earth Erdős Sweep — Turn 7

This is turn 7. Turns 1 through 6 are consolidated in `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` (attached). Re-read it before answering.
Also re-read `mega_prompt.md` and `problem_statement.md`.

CRITICAL — use ONLY the files attached to THIS message. Do NOT open, import, retrieve, or cite any file from your File Library / previously uploaded files, even one named similarly (e.g. another `turns_history…md`). Other unrelated problems use similarly named history files in the same account, and importing one will silently switch you onto the wrong problem. If the attached `turns_history__frontiermath_q2_galois.md` appears missing, do NOT continue from partial context. Reply only with `MISSING_REQUIRED_ATTACHMENT` and name the missing file(s); never substitute a library file.

If this turn drifts into hammering one specific case or sub-instance, step back and ask whether the case is revealing a structural pattern that would close many cases at once. Prefer arguments whose conclusion is a uniform statement.

End this response with ESTABLISHED / GAP / NEXT + tag.
Continue with Turn 7.

## Turn 7 — odd-index transfer closes the first essential \(C_3\)-branch

I retain Candidate \(A_2\) unchanged:

```text
sigma,tau,x0,x1

sigma2 = sigma^40491355905
u0 = (x0*tau)^40491355905
u1 = (x1*tau)^40491355905
d0 = u0*x0^-1
z0 = x0^sigma2
c0 = d0^-1*z0^-1*d0*z0

x0^2*x1^4*u1^-1*(x1^sigma)*c0
```

This is the existing \(\sigma_2\)-transvection candidate, using the prescribed idempotent \(\omega_2=40491355905\).  The previous turn isolated essential central classes over deeper non-elementary bases and specifically proposed the nonsplit natural-module extension of \(A_4\) as the first test of an odd-index transfer argument. 

### 1. The first deeper base is explicit

Let \(V\cong\mathbf F_2^2\) be the nontrivial simple \(A_4\)-module. Every nonzero class in
\[
H^2(A_4,V)\cong\mathbf F_2^2
\]
has the same abstract extension group
\[
J=(C_4\times C_4)\rtimes C_3,
\]
where a generator of \(C_3\) acts on \(C_4^2\) through
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&-1\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Indeed, if \(E=O_2(A_4)\cong C_2^2\), the preimage \(P\) of \(E\) in such an extension is a central extension
\[
1\longrightarrow V\longrightarrow P\longrightarrow E\longrightarrow1.
\]
The commutator coordinate \(\operatorname{Hom}_{C_3}(\Lambda^2E,V)\) vanishes, while the square map lies in
\[
\operatorname{End}_{C_3}(V)\cong\mathbf F_4.
\]
Every nonzero square map is invertible, forcing \(P\cong C_4^2\).

The Turn-6 lift calculation already gives
\[
|\operatorname{Sur}(A_2,J)|=384.
\]
 I independently obtained
\[
|\operatorname{Aut}(J)|=384,
\]
so \(J\) has normalized verifier count \(1\).

### 2. Odd-index determinant-transfer lemma

Let \(\Gamma\) denote either \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\) or the group presented by \(A_2\), and let
\[
\chi:\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow C_3,\qquad U=\ker\chi .
\]

For both groups,
\[
\dim H^1(U,\mathbf F_2)=5,\qquad
\dim H^2(U,\mathbf F_2)=1,
\]
and the cup product on \(H^1(U,\mathbf F_2)\) is perfect. For \(A_2\), this is the \(e=1,n=3\) specialization of the uniform tame-open calculation from Turn 4.

Because \(3\) is odd, inflation–restriction gives
\[
H^1(U,\mathbf F_2)^{C_3}\cong H^1(\Gamma,\mathbf F_2),
\]
whose dimension is \(3\). Semisimplicity of \(\mathbf F_2[C_3]\) therefore forces
\[
H^1(U,\mathbf F_2)\cong \mathbf F_2^{\,3}\oplus V,
\tag{1}
\]
with exactly one copy of the natural two-dimensional module \(V\).

Now
\[
H^1(J,\mathbf F_2)=0
\]
and, writing \(P=C_4^2\),
\[
H^2(J,\mathbf F_2)
   \cong H^2(P,\mathbf F_2)^{C_3}
   \cong \mathbf F_2.
\tag{2}
\]
The unique nonzero class \(\alpha\) restricts to
\[
\alpha|_P=u\smile v,
\tag{3}
\]
where \(u,v\) are a basis of \(H^1(P,\mathbf F_2)\cong V\). Equivalently, it is the invariant determinant form on the natural \(C_3\)-module.

Consider any epimorphism
\[
\rho:\Gamma\twoheadrightarrow J.
\]
Its restriction \(U\twoheadrightarrow P\) induces a \(C_3\)-equivariant injection
\[
H^1(P,\mathbf F_2)\hookrightarrow H^1(U,\mathbf F_2).
\]
By (1), its image is the unique \(V\)-summand.

The perfect cup pairing on \(H^1(U)\) is \(C_3\)-invariant. The trivial and \(V\)-isotypic summands are orthogonal, so perfectness forces the pairing on the \(V\)-summand to be nondegenerate. Its diagonal is zero because a \(C_3\)-invariant diagonal would define a fixed vector in \(V^\vee\). Hence
\[
\rho|_U^*(u\smile v)\ne0
      \quad\text{in }H^2(U,\mathbf F_2).
\tag{4}
\]

Finally,
\[
\operatorname{cor}\circ\operatorname{res}
   =[\,\Gamma:U\,]=3=1
   \quad\text{over }\mathbf F_2,
\]
so restriction
\[
H^2(\Gamma,\mathbf F_2)\longrightarrow H^2(U,\mathbf F_2)
\]
is injective. Consequently
\[
\rho^*\alpha\ne0
\tag{5}
\]
for **every** epimorphism \(\rho\), for both \(A_2\) and the true local Galois group.

Thus the unique nonsplit central extension
\[
1\longrightarrow C_2\longrightarrow \widetilde J
 \longrightarrow J\longrightarrow1
\]
has
\[
|\operatorname{Sur}(A_2,\widetilde J)|
=
|\operatorname{Sur}(G_{\mathbf Q_2},\widetilde J)|
=0.
\tag{6}
\]

This is pointwise obstruction control, not merely equality of aggregate counts.

### 3. The whole \(J\times C_2^r\) central tower

Since \(H^1(J,\mathbf F_2)=0\), Künneth gives
\[
H^2(J\times E,\mathbf F_2)
   =\langle\alpha\rangle\oplus\operatorname{Sym}^2(E^\vee),
   \qquad E=C_2^r.
\tag{7}
\]

The new transfer theorem says that \(\alpha\) always pulls back to the nonzero fundamental class. Every remaining coordinate is evaluated using the common Hilbert form
\[
B((a,b,c),(a',b',c'))=ac'+ca'+bb',
\]
already obtained from the maximal pro-\(2\) relation. 

There are
\[
m_r=1,7,42,168
\]
ordered independent \(r\)-tuples of quadratic characters for \(r=0,1,2,3\). Exhaustive evaluation of all quadratic forms on these frames gives:

| \(r\) | marked central classes | raw-surjection spectrum |
|---:|---:|---|
| 0 | 2 | \(1@[0],\,1@[2688]\) |
| 1 | 4 | \(1@[0],\,1@[9216],\,1@[12288],\,1@[16128]\) |
| 2 | 16 | \(1@[0],\,7@[55296],\,1@[64512],\,7@[73728]\) |
| 3 | 128 | \(2@[0],\,63@[221184],\,63@[294912]\) |

Here \(m@[N]\) means \(m\) extension classes have \(N\) raw surjections.

Thus the odd-index argument closes **all 150 marked central \(C_2\)-extension classes over \(J\times C_2^r\)**, not just the original order-\(96\) cover. The \(r=3\) split class also has zero surjections because a fourth independent quadratic character cannot exist.

The finite certificate constructs \(J\), its unique nonsplit central cover, exhaustively obtains \(384\) and \(0\), computes \(|\operatorname{Aut}(J)|=384\), and reproduces all four spectra:

[Turn 7 odd-index transfer certificate](sandbox:/mnt/data/turn7_odd_index_transfer_certificate.py)

**ESTABLISHED**: Proved a multiplicity-one odd-index transfer criterion for determinant-type essential central classes. Applied it to the nonsplit natural-module extension \(J=(C_4^2)\rtimes C_3\) of \(A_4\): \(J\) has raw count \(384\), normalized count \(1\), while its unique nonsplit central \(C_2\)-cover has count \(0\) for both Candidate \(A_2\) and \(G_{\mathbf Q_2}\). Propagated this pointwise obstruction through every central \(C_2\)-extension of \(J\times C_2^r\), closing 150 marked classes.

**GAP**: The official 5402-group verifier remains unavailable. The transfer argument currently controls classes whose odd-index restriction is a cup-determinant on a multiplicity-one simple summand. Essential invariant classes restricting to indecomposable elements of \(H^2(P,\mathbf F_2)\), and the nonnormal index-three Sylow restrictions in the \(S_3\)-branches, remain untreated.

**NEXT**: Extend the criterion from normal \(C_3\)-kernels to arbitrary odd-index subgroups using restriction–corestriction and Mackey conjugacy. Apply it simultaneously to the Sylow-\(2\) subgroups of the nonsplit \(V\)-extensions of \(S_4\), \(\mathrm{GL}_2(3)\), and \(2O\), classifying their invariant \(H^2\)-coordinates into determinant-detectable and genuinely indecomposable parts.

[BREAKTHROUGH]
